title: Lesson 12: The Past Tense: 'Be' & All Verb Classes   تیر زمان: 'وي' او ټولې کریاوې
description: Cross a major grammatical milestone by mastering the Pashto Past Tense. You will learn the past forms of the verb 'to be', all verb classes, and essential medical vocabulary grounded in the cultural tradition of visiting the sick.

content.heading: Module 1: The Past Tense of 'To Be'
lines: Welcome to Unit 12! This unit introduces a major grammatical milestone: the Pashto Past Tense[cite: 58].
We begin with the past form of the copula 'to be', which has both an imperfective form ([wəw-]) and a perfective form ([wəw-]), alongside a highly irregular paradigm[cite: 58].

The past tense introduces an entirely new set of personal endings for the 3rd person[cite: 58]. Unlike the present tense, the past tense 3rd person endings are: masculine singular [a], plural [a]; feminine singular [a], plural [e][cite: 58].
Sentence Examples: احمد په کور کې وو. [Ahmad kor ke wəw.] (Ahmad was at home.)[cite: 58].
Negative Past: احمد په کور کې نه وو. [Ahmad kor ke nəwəw.] (Ahmad was not at home.)[cite: 58].

content.text: Phonetics Hack: When pronouncing the past tense of the copula [wəw-], note that the initial [wə-] is the past marker itself, NOT the perfective prefix you learned in Unit 7[cite: 59]. Also, a key morphophonological rule to remember: the past suffix [əl] reduces to [əw] for the 3rd person singular masculine[cite: 59].

content.heading: Module 2: Medical & Body Vocabulary
lines: Our communicative context for learning the past tense is narrating past events and visiting a sick person[cite: 57, 58].
Essential Diagnostic Phrase: چېرې درد کوي؟ [čere dard kawe] (Where does it hurt?)[cite: 58].

content.text: Cultural Protocol: In Pashtun culture, visiting the sick (known as marīzpursi) is not just a nice gesture; it is an obligatory community duty[cite: 58]. Visitors traditionally bring food and recite Quranic du'a (prayers) for the ill person[cite: 58]. Sociolinguistic Taboo: Strict gender norms apply—women visit women, and men visit men, with exceptions made only for close family[cite: 58].

content.question: You are invited to participate in 'marīzpursi' (visiting the sick) for a colleague in a traditional Afghan community. What is a critical cultural and sociolinguistic rule you must follow?
options: You must speak only in the past perfective tense.
You should bring medicine and administer it yourself.
You must observe traditional gender norms, meaning men visit men and women visit women unless they are close family.
You should avoid asking where it hurts to respect their privacy.
content.explanation: Marīzpursi is a socially mandatory practice that strictly adheres to gender norms; women visit women, and men visit men, except for close family[cite: 58].

content.heading: Module 3: Past Tense Across Verb Classes
lines: Now we will apply the past tense to regular, derivative, and irregular verbs[cite: 58].
Regular simple past: ده کتاب ووایه. [Da kitāb wəwayəl.] (He read the book — past perfective)[cite: 58].
Derivative past: کور تود کړی. [Kor tōd kṛəy.] (The house was warmed)[cite: 58].
Irregular Past Stems: Many simple verbs have suppletive roots in the past tense that are completely unrelated to their present stems[cite: 58].
Examples: [tləl] (to go) becomes [lāṛ shəw-]; [rātləl] (to come) becomes [rāghəl-]; [kṛāwəl] becomes [wakər-][cite: 58].

content.heading: The Master Paradigm Table
lines: In your textbook, you will find a critical 4-column master paradigm table comparing: Present Imperfective | Present Perfective | Past Imperfective | Past Perfective[cite: 58].
This table covers regular verbs, derivative verbs, and 5 key irregular verbs[cite: 58].

Note: In accordance with our script policy, this master paradigm table is presented exclusively in full Pashto script[cite: 59]. This is the reference anchor for the rest of your curriculum[cite: 58].

content.question: When constructing sentences in the Pashto past tense, what entirely new set of endings must you use for the 3rd person (he/she/it/they) compared to the present tense?
options: Masculine takes [ay], feminine takes [a], and plural takes [i].
Masculine singular takes [a], plural takes [a]; feminine singular takes [a], plural takes [e].
The endings do not change; only the past prefix [wə-] is added.
All 3rd person past tense forms end in [əl].
content.explanation: The past tense introduces a distinct personal ending set in the 3rd person: masculine singular [a], plural [a]; feminine singular [a], plural [e][cite: 58].
